content:
The biggest AI skills gap nobody talks about

8 min read
1 hour ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Spend five minutes on LinkedIn and you might think the entire technology industry has collectively decided to become an AI engineer.
Everywhere you look, people are learning prompt engineering. They’re building AI agents, experimenting with retrieval-augmented generation, comparing large language models, and sharing screenshots of chatbots they built over the weekend. New AI courses appear daily. New frameworks launch weekly. New “complete AI roadmaps” arrive with the consistency of weather forecasts.
None of this is a bad thing.
AI is genuinely transforming how software gets built, how businesses operate, and how knowledge workers spend their time. Learning AI today is one of the smartest investments a developer can make.
Yet after spending the past few years building with AI, experimenting with GenAI tools, and watching thousands of people attempt to enter the field, I’ve noticed something interesting.
Everyone seems focused on learning AI.
Almost nobody is focused on learning how to think.
That may sound dramatic, so let me explain.
Because in my experience, the biggest AI skills gap in the industry is not prompt engineering, machine learning, model selection, or knowing which framework happens to be trending this month.
The biggest AI skills gap is systems thinking.
And the longer I work in this space, the more convinced I become that it separates successful AI builders from everyone else.
The strange thing that happens when AI becomes easier
One of the most fascinating side effects of modern AI tools is that they have dramatically reduced the effort required to perform many technical tasks. A few years ago, generating a Python script, writing a SQL query, creating documentation, or producing an initial architecture draft required significantly more manual work. Today, an AI assistant can help with all of those tasks in seconds.
That shift is genuinely remarkable. It has lowered barriers to entry, accelerated development cycles, and allowed developers to move much faster than before. However, it has also created a subtle change that many people overlook. As execution becomes easier, decision-making becomes more important.
In the past, developers spent much of their time figuring out how to implement something. Increasingly, AI can assist with implementation. The bottleneck is moving elsewhere.
Today, the more valuable questions often look like this:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Notice something interesting. The questions on the right are not primarily coding questions. They are design questions. They require understanding relationships, dependencies, trade-offs, and long-term consequences.
This is why I believe one of the most important AI skills today has very little to do with prompting. As AI tools become more capable, the ability to think about systems becomes increasingly valuable. The people who understand how all the pieces fit together will often create far more value than those who simply know how to operate the tools.
What systems thinking actually means
The phrase systems thinking sounds suspiciously like something a consultant would write on a whiteboard before sending an invoice large enough to make everyone uncomfortable.
In reality, the concept is surprisingly practical.
Systems thinking is the ability to understand how multiple components interact to produce an outcome. Instead of evaluating individual pieces in isolation, you focus on the relationships between them. Rather than asking whether one component works, you ask whether the entire system works.
Consider a simple AI chatbot. Most beginners immediately focus on the model. They compare GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, and open-source alternatives, trying to determine which one will produce the best results. Those discussions matter, but they often miss a much larger picture.
An AI chatbot is rarely just a model. It is usually a combination of prompts, retrieval systems, APIs, databases, memory layers, user interfaces, monitoring tools, evaluation frameworks, authentication systems, latency constraints, and error-handling mechanisms.
The table below illustrates the difference:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once you begin viewing AI products through this lens, your priorities start to shift. You spend less time obsessing over individual technologies and more time understanding how technologies work together. That change alone can dramatically improve your effectiveness as an AI engineer.
Why this AI skills gap keeps growing
Part of the reason this gap continues to widen is that most educational content naturally focuses on things that are easy to teach.
Courses teach frameworks.
Tutorials teach workflows.
Videos teach implementation.
Documentation teaches features.
All of those resources are valuable. The problem is that systems thinking is much harder to package into a ten-minute tutorial.
Nobody is rushing to upload a viral video titled:
“Watch Me Analyze Architectural Trade-Offs for the Next 45 Minutes.”
At least, I sincerely hope nobody is.
As a result, many learners become proficient at individual technologies before developing a mental model for how those technologies fit together. They know what a vector database is because they watched three tutorials about it. They know what an AI agent is because they built one during a weekend project.
Yet when faced with a real business problem, they struggle with a more important question:
Should I use these technologies at all?
That distinction matters.
The issue is rarely knowledge. Most developers today have access to more information than ever before.
The issue is judgment.
Knowing what a technology does is useful. Knowing when it should be used, why it should be used, and whether a simpler solution would be better is where experience starts to emerge.
The AI engineers who stand out
Whenever people ask me how to become an AI engineer, they usually expect a conversation about frameworks, certifications, model providers, or programming languages. Those topics certainly matter, but they are rarely what separates good engineers from exceptional ones.
The strongest AI engineers I know share a different characteristic: they consistently ask better questions.
When presented with a problem, they immediately start evaluating trade-offs rather than jumping directly into implementation. They think about reliability, cost, scalability, maintainability, and user experience long before they think about which framework they plan to import.
Some of the questions they routinely ask include:

• What happens if the model produces an incorrect answer?

• How will the system behave under heavy load?

• What are the latency requirements?

• How expensive will inference become at scale?

• What happens if an external API becomes unavailable?

• Could a simpler solution achieve the same outcome?
None of these questions are particularly flashy. You probably will not see them trending on social media next to screenshots of the latest AI demo.
However, these are the questions that determine whether a product succeeds in the real world.
Anyone can follow a tutorial and recreate a project. Far fewer people can design systems that remain useful, reliable, and maintainable after real users start interacting with them.
Why AI skills alone are no longer enough
There was a period when simply knowing how to use AI tools provided a meaningful competitive advantage. If you understood prompting, knew how to work with APIs, and could build basic AI-powered applications, you were already ahead of a large percentage of the market.
That advantage is shrinking rapidly.
Today, millions of developers have access to the same models, the same documentation, the same frameworks, and the same tutorials. Generating code is becoming easier. Building prototypes is becoming faster. Learning new tools is becoming more accessible.
The differentiator is increasingly shifting away from tool usage and toward problem-solving.
Organizations are not hiring people because they know how to open ChatGPT. They are hiring people because they can solve business problems, design workflows, improve processes, and create systems that deliver value. AI is becoming part of that process, but it is not replacing the need for thoughtful decision-making.
That is why I believe systems thinking will become one of the most important AI skills over the next decade. The value is moving away from operating the tools and toward understanding how those tools fit into larger systems.
How to develop systems thinking
The good news is that systems thinking can be learned.
The less exciting news is that it usually cannot be learned through passive consumption alone.
Reading helps. Courses help. Tutorials help. However, systems thinking tends to develop when you encounter messy real-world problems that do not have clean answers.
This is one of the reasons I consistently recommend project-based learning. Projects force you to think beyond individual technologies because they introduce questions that tutorials rarely address.
When you build something real, you quickly start asking:

• How should data move through the application?

• What happens when an API fails?

• How should errors be surfaced to users?

• What should be monitored?

• How will this scale?

• How can the experience be improved?
Those questions naturally push you toward systems thinking because they require considering the entire ecosystem rather than a single component.
Structured learning paths can also be useful. Resources like DeepLearning.AI provide excellent foundational knowledge, while platforms such as Fenzo.ai can help organize learning around specific goals instead of isolated concepts. What I appreciate about these approaches is that they encourage learners to connect ideas rather than simply collect them.
Ultimately, the goal is not accumulating more facts. The goal is developing a mental model that helps you understand how everything fits together.
The future belongs to builders who can connect the dots
One mistake I see repeatedly is people treating AI as a collection of disconnected tools.
In reality, AI is gradually becoming infrastructure.
Just as modern software engineers eventually needed to understand databases, APIs, cloud platforms, authentication systems, and distributed architectures, future AI engineers will need to understand how models interact with larger systems.
The people who thrive in this environment will not necessarily be those who memorize the most frameworks or experiment with every new model release. More often, they will be the builders who can connect seemingly unrelated pieces into coherent solutions.
Technical knowledge remains important. AI skills remain important. Understanding models, prompts, and frameworks still matters.
But the ability to see the bigger picture, to understand how all those components work together, is what turns knowledge into leverage.
And that is exactly why systems thinking continues to be one of the most overlooked AI skills in the industry today.
Final thoughts
Whenever someone asks me which AI skills they should focus on, they are usually expecting a list of technologies. Prompt engineering, RAG, AI agents, fine-tuning, model evaluation, and dozens of other topics tend to dominate the conversation.
Those skills absolutely matter. In many cases, they are worth learning.
However, if I could recommend only one thing to someone entering the field today, it would not be a framework or a model.
It would be learning how to think in systems.
Learn how data flows through applications. Learn how components interact. Learn how failures propagate. Learn how users behave. Learn how technical decisions influence business outcomes.
Because as AI tools become increasingly accessible, the ability to operate those tools will become common. The ability to understand
